topic_title: Confusion matrix — why 95% accuracy can still mean frequent failure
verdict: PASSED — all 6 checks clear. Citation density: [1] present on every factual paragraph in §5 and §6; ≥60% coverage confirmed in both sections. Sequence drift: 'ground truth' and 'positive class' now in 7.10's concepts_introduced; both defined on first use with explicit bold-term + plain-language blocks; 'negative class' defined in the same sentence as 'positive class' at §5.1; 'threshold' removed entirely. Length: estimated ~3200 words, within standard corpus range [1500–4000]. Scope: confusion matrix + four cells + accuracy paradox only; F1/ROC/AUC/sensitivity/specificity absent; no scope creep. Readability: all three cycle-1 undefined-term findings resolved; 0 distinct soft findings; no jargon dumps; terms defined on first use throughout.

findings.detail: Section 5.6, line 192: the bridging sentence 'Why does TN matter, then? Because TN is what inflates accuracy in imbalanced datasets (as shown in section 5.4).' lacks a [1] marker, but the surrounding sentences in the same paragraph carry [1]. Coverage in §5.6 remains above 60%. Not a hard fail.

cycle1_resolutions.resolution: Explicit bold-term definition added at §5.1 (line 53): 'Ground truth — the actual correct label for each item in the dataset, determined by a human expert or a reliable reference before the model is run.' Used consistently thereafter.

Explicit bold-term definitions added at §5.1 (line 51): 'Positive class — the category the model is trained to detect' with concrete spam/disease examples; negative class defined as 'everything that is not the positive class — the default, unremarkable outcome the model is trying to separate from.' Both carry [1].
Removed entirely from corpus. No occurrence found.
